result:
There is a specific kind of silence that exists at the bottom of the ocean, a silence so profound it feels like a physical weight. Down there, miles beneath the sun’s fading reach, the water doesn't crash or ripple; it merely exists, pressing in with the heavy patience of millennia. Bioluminescent creatures drift like fallen stars, blinking in the dark, scripting their own lonely language of light. They have no idea about the world above—the roaring winds, the towering cities, the frantic scurry of human ambition. They only know the pressure, the cold, and the slow, endless drift of marine snow falling like ash from a world they will never visit.

I find a strange comfort in that. Down in the aphotic zone, time is measured not in seconds or years, but in the shifting of tectonic plates and the rare fall of a whale carcass. It is a reminder that most of the universe is quiet, untouched, and profoundly indifferent to our noise. We spend our lives racing against the clock, desperate to leave a mark, building monuments to our own existence, while the deep ocean holds its breath and simply waits. It doesn't need to be remembered. It doesn't need to be seen. It just is—a vast, dark cathedral of water, holding the earth together in its cold embrace. Somewhere right now, in that crushing dark, a strange, gelatinous fish is glowing, entirely unaware of how beautiful it is.
